what_it_does: Defines the 'platform' UI-contract resource: a ledger entry that maps host/org-level Claude Code concerns (install/update, enterprise admin, cloud providers, large-codebase setup, privacy/security, glossary) to their native models, marks each as building, planned, or out-of-local-scope with a reason, and declares the tiny slice of platform reality the company fabric can actually observe or route to.
observations: A refocus note says the previous command-wrapper endpoints (MCP tools, /api config bridge, R3 config_writer rail) were removed; the real capability is driving an already-installed Claude Code session.
Platform concerns are identified by CLASS, not a fabric address; there is no platform:// scheme.
The schema only admits claude_version, claude_bin, and fabric_env as company-observable platform facts.
A table maps Feature-Atlas classes CC-28/29/31/32/34/35 to native models and marks their reality status.
Only CC-35 (glossary) is 'building' and realized as a knowledge-corpus search over claude-code-atlas.
Most platform ops (install, enterprise, cloud-provider, privacy) are documented as returning platform.out-of-local-scope (HTTP 501) with native recovery paths.
platform.data-posture is a reopened CC-32 write for the operator's own data-posture env flags, but the wrapper write path was removed and it is now honestly planned via driving a real session.
The file uses contract:schema, contract:op, contract:error, and contract:example blocks consistently.
It explicitly states the company's own local persistence boundary: session records + mail in STORE_DIR, 127.0.0.1 only, no Anthropic credential, no inference proxy.
Relates-to lists auth, settings, diagnostics, surfaces, session, cost-usage, and knowledge-corpus.
standouts: The file is mostly a negative map: it documents what the fabric deliberately does NOT do and where the real responsibility lies.
The only built platform read is the glossary, implemented as a lens on knowledge-corpus search.
platform.data-posture is the single reopened operator-level write, yet it currently has no binding because the wrapper was removed.
It invents an explicit 501 error vocabulary (platform.out-of-local-scope) with per-class recovery instructions.
conventions: Follows contract-entry frontmatter with type, resource, summary, schemes, status, relates-to.
References related resources with [[...]] wiki-style links.
Marks operations with status values like planned and building.
Employs 'absence-shaped' consequences and honest refusal language.
Breaks typical API-contract norms by devoting most ops to non-operations and 501 responses.
concerns: platform.data-posture still describes an R3 config_writer MCP example that was removed, so that section may be stale.
op: platform.privacy lists atlas [CC-31.1], which is the large-codebase class, while the prose is about privacy/security (CC-32); this looks like a mismatch.
platform.privacy bundles privacy/security and large-codebase setup into one op, which muddles the class boundary.
Several items marked 'planned' are explicitly declared out-of-local-scope, which could be confusing.
Examples are synthetic and labeled as such, reducing verifiability.
notes: A scope-honesty document: it systematically tells you which platform/admin/host tasks the fabric refuses to do and where to go instead, while keeping only the glossary lookup and a future data-posture write on the company side.
questions: Is platform.data-posture reachable today in any form, or is it blocked until a future 'drive real session' path is defined?
Is the atlas reference [CC-31.1] in platform.privacy a typo for [CC-32.1]?
How does the fabric actually read claude_version from the session system/init event, since that event is not shown here?
What is the canonical source for the closed data-posture flag set, and are DISABLE_BUG_COMMAND and CLAUDE_CODE_DISABLE_NONESSENTIAL_TRAFFIC official keys?
Does the company plan to expose a settings.env read op, or is the data-posture 'get' meant to be done through settings directly?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: it maps platform classes to native models and marks scope. It also adds the deliberate pattern of documenting non-operations with 501 errors and a reopened but unbuilt data-posture write.
apparent_themes: Honest scope boundary and refusal of out-of-local-scope work.
No silent gaps: every class is reachable in navigation even if not implemented.
Prefer driving a real Claude Code session over fabric-specific wrappers.
intention_signals: The file is trying to stop consumers from asking the fabric to install, update, administer orgs, switch cloud providers, or set retention, and instead route them to the host/org native paths; it keeps only glossary lookup and a personal data-posture toggle on the company side.
novelty: A contract resource whose primary content is documenting capabilities the system intentionally does not implement, complete with explicit 501 errors, native recovery paths, and 'absence-shaped' evidence.
contribution: It contributes an honest scope map, a reusable out-of-local-scope error vocabulary with per-class recovery guidance, the glossary-as-knowledge-corpus lens, an explicit statement of the company's local data boundary, and a closed set of data-posture flags for future operator control.
summary_for_embedding: The platform UI-contract resource maps host- and org-level Claude Code concerns to their native models, declares most of them out-of-local-scope with 501 recovery paths, and keeps only the glossary lookup and a planned operator data-posture write on the company fabric.
intention_for_embedding: This file exists to prevent silent gaps by making every platform class navigable while honestly refusing host/org administration, routing real work to the host install or Anthropic org.
